title: American buttermilk biscuits
subtitle: Flaky, layered, savoury biscuits
excerpt: American biscuits are not sweet biscuits but a type of quick bread: tall, flaky, and tender. Made with cold butter, buttermilk, and plain flour, they are mixed minimally, folded to build layers, and baked in a very hot oven.
sourceNotes: American biscuits are a Southern US staple with roots in the early nineteenth century. Fannie Farmer, The Boston Cooking-School Cook Book (1896), gives a baking powder biscuit recipe consistent with the modern form. The buttermilk version, associated with Southern home baking, appears in American domestic manuals from the late nineteenth century. The technique of laminating the dough by folding to create layers is a refinement documented across twentieth-century American baking literature.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze baked biscuits. Defrost and reheat in a 180°C oven for 5 minutes.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Best eaten hot from the oven. They soften as they cool.

content.text: American biscuits rise on steam: the cold butter in the dough turns to steam in the hot oven, pushing the layers apart. Everything about the method is designed to keep the butter cold and the dough unworked. Grated frozen butter distributes evenly without warming from the hands. Folding the dough in half three times (like folding a letter) builds distinct layers without overmixing. Bake at 230°C for the rapid steam burst that gives the lift.

Heat the oven to 230°C fan. Line a baking tray with baking paper. Whisk the flour, baking powder, and salt in a large bowl. Grate the frozen butter directly into the flour and toss to coat the strands. Pour in the cold buttermilk and stir with a fork until the dough just comes together. It will be shaggy and uneven. Turn onto a lightly floured surface.

Pat the dough into a rough rectangle about 2 cm thick. Fold it in thirds like a letter, rotate 90 degrees, and repeat twice more. Pat out to 3 cm thick. Cut rounds with a 6 cm cutter, pressing straight down without twisting. Place on the prepared tray with sides touching for a soft pull-apart finish. Bake for 12 to 14 minutes, until risen and deep golden on top. Serve immediately.
